title: Detecting danger in gridworlds using Gromov’s Link Condition [PDF]

## **Detecting danger in gridworlds using Gromov’s Link Condition**

## **Anonymous Author(s)**

Affiliation Address `email`

## **Abstract**

1 Gridworlds have been long-utilised in AI research, particularly in reinforcement 2 learning, as they provide simple yet scalable models for many real-world applica3 tions such as robot navigation, emergent behaviour, and operations research. We 4 initiate a study of gridworlds using the mathematical framework of _reconfigurable_ 5 _systems_ and _state complexes_ due to Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson. State complexes 6 represent all possible configurations of a system as a single geometric space, thus 7 making them conducive to study using geometric, topological, or combinatorial 8 methods. The main contribution of this work is a modification to the original 9 Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson setup which we introduce to capture agent braiding and 10 thereby more naturally represent the topology of gridworlds. With this modification, 11 the state complexes may exhibit geometric defects (failure of _Gromov’s Link Condi-_ 12 _tion_ ). Serendipitously, we discover these failures occur exactly where undesirable 13 or dangerous states appear in the gridworld. Our results therefore provide a novel 14 method for seeking guaranteed safety limitations in discrete task environments 15 with single or multiple agents, and offer useful safety information (in geometric 16 and topological forms) for incorporation in or analysis of machine learning sys17 tems. More broadly, our work introduces tools from geometric group theory and 18 combinatorics to the AI community and demonstrates a proof-of-concept for this 19 geometric viewpoint of the task domain through the example of simple gridworld 20 environments.

## 21 **1 Introduction**

- 22 The notion of a state (or configuration/phase) space is commonly used in mathematics and physics to 23 represent all the possible states of a given system as a single geometric (or topological) object. This 24 perspective provides a bridge which allows for tools from geometry and topology to be applied to 25 the system of concern. Moreover, certain features of a given system are reflected by some geometric 26 aspects of the associated state space (such as gravitational force being captured by _curvature_ in 27 spacetime). Thus, insights into the structure of the original system can be gleaned by reformulating 28 them in geometric terms.

- 29 In discrete settings, state spaces are typically represented by graphs or their higher dimensional 30 analogues such as simplicial complexes or cube complexes. Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson’s _state_ 31 _complexes_ [AG04, GP07] provide a general framework for representing discrete reconfigurable 32 systems as non-positively curved (NPC) cube complexes, giving access to a wealth of mathematical 33 and computational benefits via efficient optimisation algorithms guided by geometric insight [AOS12]. 34 These have been used to develop efficient algorithms for robotic motion planning [ABY14, ABCG17] 35 and self-reconfiguration of modular robots [LR10]. NPC cube complexes also possess rich hyperplane 36 structures which geometrically capture binary classification [CN05, Wis12, Sag14]. However, their 37 broader utility to fields like artificial intelligence (AI) has until now been relatively unexplored.
Submitted to 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). Do not distribute.

- 38 Our main contribution is the first application of this geometric approach (of using state complexes) 39 to the setting of multi-agent gridworlds. We introduce a natural modification to the state complex 40 appropriate to the setting of gridworlds (to capture the braiding or relative movements of agents); 41 however, this can lead to state complexes which are no longer NPC. Nevertheless, by applying 42 Gromov’s Link Condition, we completely characterise when positive curvature occurs in our new 43 state complexes, and relate this to features of the gridworlds (see Theorem 5.2). Serendipitously, 44 we discover that the states where Gromov’s Link Condition fails are those in which agents can 45 potentially collide. In other words, collision-detection is naturally embedded into the intrinsic 46 geometry of the system. Current approaches to collision-detection and navigation during multi47 agent navigation often rely on modelling and predicting collisions based on large training datasets 48 [KFGE19, FLLP20, QZC[+] 21] or by explicitly modelling physical movements [KIU21]. However, 49 our approach is purely geometric, requires no training, and can accommodate many conceivable types 50 of actions and inter-actions, not just simple movements.

- 51 Our work relates to a growing body of research aimed towards understanding, from a geometric 52 perspective, how deep learning methods transform input data into decisions, memories, or actions 53 [HR17, LAG[+] 20, SPG[+] 21, AVBP21, SMK11]. However, such studies do not usually incorporate 54 the geometry of the originating domain or task in a substantial way, before applying or investigating 55 the performance of learning algorithms – and even fewer do so for multi-agent systems. One possible 56 reason for this is a lack of known suitable tools. Our experimental and theoretical results show there 57 is a wealth of geometric information available in (even very simple) task domains, which is accessible 58 using tools from geometric group theory and combinatorics.

## 59 **2 State complex of a gridworld**

- 60 A _gridworld_ is a two-dimensional, flat array of _cells_ arranged in a grid, 61 much like a chess or checker board. Each cell can be occupied or un62 occupied. A cell may be occupied, in our setting, by one and only 63 one freely-moving agent or movable object. Other gridworlds may in64 clude rewards, punishments, buttons, doors, locks, keys, checkpoints, 65 dropbears, etc., much like many basic video games. Gridworlds have 66 been a long-utilised setting in AI research, particularly reinforcement 67 learning, since they are simple yet scalable in size and sophistication 68 [DSHLKT20, WKK20]. They also offer clear analogies to many real69 world applications or questions, such as robot navigation [HHA21], emer70 gent behaviour [KAP20], and operations research [LSS[+] 21]. For these 71 reasons, gridworlds have also been developed for formally specifying 72 problems in AI safety [LMK[+] 17].

- 73 A _state_ of a gridworld can be encoded by assigning each cell a _label_ . In 74 the example shown in Figure 1, these labels are shown for an agent, an 75 object, and empty floor. A change in the state, such as an agent moving 76 from one cell to an adjacent empty cell, can be encoded by _relabelling_ the

Figure 1: A 3 _×_ 3 gridworld with one agent (a koala) and one object (a beach ball).

- 77 cells involved. This perspective allows us to take advantage of the notion of _reconfigurable systems_ 78 as introduced by Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson [AG04, GP07].

- 79 More formally, consider a graph _G_ and a set _A_ of labels. A _state_ is a function _s_ : _V_ ( _G_ ) _→A_ , i.e. an 80 assignment of a label to each vertex of _G_ . A possible relabelling is encoded using a _generator ϕ_ ; this 81 comprises the following data:

82

83

- a subgraph _SUP_ ( _ϕ_ ) _⊆ G_ called the _support_ ;

- a subgraph _TR_ ( _ϕ_ ) _⊆ SUP_ ( _ϕ_ ) called the _trace_ ; and

- 84 • an unordered pair of _local states_

85 that agree on _V_ ( _SUP_ ( _ϕ_ )) _− V_ ( _TR_ ( _ϕ_ )) but differ on _V_ ( _TR_ ( _ϕ_ )).

86 A generator _ϕ_ is _admissible_ at a state _s_ if _s|SUP_ ( _ϕ_ ) = _u[loc]_ 0 (or _u[loc]_ 1[), in other words, if the assignment] 87 of labels to _V_ ( _SUP_ ( _ϕ_ )) given by _s_ completely matches the labelling from (exactly) one of the two

88 local states. If this holds, we may apply _ϕ_ to the state _s_ to obtain a new state _ϕ_ [ _s_ ] given by

- 89 This has the effect of relabelling the vertices in (and only in) _TR_ ( _ϕ_ ) to match the other local state 90 of _ϕ_ . Since the local states are unordered, if _ϕ_ is admissible at _s_ then it is also admissible at _ϕ_ [ _s_ ]; 91 moreover, _ϕ_ [ _ϕ_ [ _s_ ]] = _s_ .

- 92 **Definition 2.1** (Reconfigurable system [AG04, GP07]) **.** A _reconfigurable system_ on a graph _G_ with 93 a set of labels _A_ consists of a set of generators together with a set of states closed under the action of 94 admissible generators.

- 95 Configurations and their reconfigurations can be used to construct a _state graph_ (or transition graph), 96 which represents all possible states and transitions between these states in a reconfigurable system. 97 More formally:

- 98 **Definition 2.2** (State graph) **.** The state graph _S_[(1)] associated to a reconfigurable system has as its 99 vertices the set of all states, with edges connecting pairs of states differing by a single generator.

100 Let us now return our attention to gridworlds. We define a graph _G_ to have vertices corresponding to 101 the cells of a gridworld, with two vertices declared adjacent in _G_ exactly when they correspond to 102 neighbouring cells (i.e. they share a common side). Our set of labels is chosen to be
- 103 We do not distinguish between multiple instances of the same label. We consider two generators:
- 104 • **Push/Pull.** An agent adjacent to an object is allowed to push/pull the object if there is an 105 unoccupied floor cell straight in front of the object/straight behind the agent; and

- 106 • **Move.** An agent is allowed to move to a neighbouring unoccupied floor cell.

- 107 These two generators have the effect of enabling agents to at any time move in any direction not 108 blocked by objects or other agents, and for agents to push or pull objects within the environment into 109 any configuration if there is sufficient room to move. For both types of generators, the trace coincides 110 with the support. For the Push/Pull generator, the support is a row or column of three contiguous 111 cells, whereas for the Move generator, the support is a pair of neighbouring cells. A simple example 112 of a state graph, together with the local states for the two generator types, is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 2: An example 1 _×_ 5 gridworld with one agent and one object with two generators – Push/Pull and Move – and the resulting state graph. In the state graph, edge colours indicate the generator type which relabels the gridworld.

- 113 In a typical reconfigurable system, there may be many admissible generators at a given state _s_ . If the 114 trace of an admissible generator _ϕ_ 1 is disjoint from the support of another admissible generator _ϕ_ 2,
- 115 then _ϕ_ 2 remains admissible at _ϕ_ 1[ _s_ ]. This is because the relabelling by _ϕ_ 1 does not interfere with

3

116 the labels on _SUP_ ( _ϕ_ 2). More generally, a set of admissible generators _{ϕ_ 1 _, . . . , ϕn}_ at a state _s_ 117 _commutes_ if _SUP_ ( _ϕi_ ) _∩ TR_ ( _ϕj_ ) = _∅_ for all _i ̸_ = _j_ . When this holds, these generators can be applied 118 independently of one another, and the resulting state does not depend on the order in which they are 119 applied. A simple example of this in the context of gridworlds is a large room with _n_ agents spread 120 sufficiently far apart to allow for independent simultaneous movement.

- 121 Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson represent this mutual 122 commutativity by adding higher dimensional 123 cubes to the state graph to form a cube com124 plex called the _state complex_ . We give an infor125 mal definition here, and refer to their papers for 126 the precise formulation [AG04, GP07]. Further 127 background on cube complexes can be found 128 in [Wis12, Sag14]. If _{ϕ_ 1 _, . . . , ϕn}_ is a set of 129 commuting admissible generators at a state _s_ 130 then there are 2 _[n]_ states that can be obtained 131 by applying any subset of these generators to _s_ . 132 These 2 _[n]_ states form the vertices of an _n_ –cube 133 in the state complex. Each _n_ –cube is bounded 134 by 2 _n_ faces, where each face is an ( _n −_ 1)–cube: 135 by disallowing a generator _ϕi_ , we obtain a pair 136 of faces corresponding to those states (in the 137 given _n_ –cube) that agree with one of the two 138 respective local states of _ϕi_ on _SUP_ ( _ϕi_ ).
Figure 3: State complex of a 2 _×_ 2 gridworld with two agents. Shading indicates squares attached to the surrounding 4–cycles.

- 139 **Definition 2.3** (State complex) **.** The _state complex S_ of a reconfigurable system is the cube complex 140 constructed from the state graph _S_[(1)] by inductively adding cubes as follows: whenever there is a 141 set of 2 _[n]_ states related by a set of _n_ admissible commuting generators, we add an _n_ –cube so that its 142 vertices correspond to the given states, and so that its 2 _n_ boundary faces are identified with all the 143 possible ( _n −_ 1)–cubes obtained by disallowing a generator. In particular, every cube is uniquely 144 determined by its vertices.

- 145 In our gridworlds setting, each generator involves exactly one agent. This means commuting 146 generators can only occur if there are multiple agents. A simple example of a state complex for 147 two agents in a 2 _×_ 2 room is shown in Figure 3. Note that there are six embedded 4–cycles in the 148 state graph, however, only two of these are filled in by squares: these correspond to independent 149 movements of the agents, either both horizontally or both vertically.

## **3 Exploring gridworlds with state complexes**

- 151 To compute the state complex of a (finite) gridworld, we first initialise an empty graph _G_ and an 152 empty ‘to-do’ list _L_ . As input, we take a chosen state of the gridworld to form the first vertex of _G_ 153 and also the first entry on _L_ . The state complex is computed according to a breadth-first search by 154 repeatedly applying the following:

155

- Let _v_ be the first entry on _L_ . List all admissible generators at _v_ . For each such generator _ϕ_ :
- 156 **–** If _ϕ_ [ _v_ ] already appears as a vertex of _G_ , add an edge between _v_ and _ϕ_ [ _v_ ] (if it does not 157 already exist).
- 158 **–** If _ϕ_ [ _v_ ] does not appear in _G_ , add it as a new vertex to _G_ and add an edge connecting it 159 to _v_ . Append _ϕ_ [ _v_ ] to the end of _L_ .

- 160 • Remove _v_ from _L_ .

- 161 The process terminates when _L_ is empty. The output is the graph _G_ . When _L_ is empty, we have 162 fully explored all possible states that can be reached from the initial state. It may be possible that 163 the true state graph is disconnected, in which case the above algorithm will only return a connected 164 component _G_ . For our purposes, we shall limit our study to systems with connected state graphs. 165 From the state graph, we construct the state complex by first finding all 4–cycles in the state graph.
- 166 Then, by examining the states involved, we can determine whether a given 4–cycle bounds a square 167 representing a pair of commuting moves.

4

- 168 To visualise the state complex, we first draw the state graph using the Kamada–Kawai force-directed 169 algorithm [KK89] which attempts to draw edges to have similar length. We then shade the region(s) 170 enclosed by 4–cycles representing commuting moves. For ease of visual interpretation in our figures, 171 we do not also shade higher-dimensional cubes, although such cubes are noticeable and can be easily 172 computed and visualised if desired.

- 173 Constructing and analysing state com174 plexes of gridworlds is in and of itself an in175 teresting and useful way of exploring their 176 intrinsic geometry. For example, Figure 4 177 shows the state complex of a 3 _×_ 3 grid178 world with one agent and one object. The 179 state complex reveals two scales of geom180 etry: larger ‘blobs’ of states organised in 181 a 3 _×_ 3 grid, representing the location of 182 the object; and, within each blob, copies 183 of the room’s remaining empty space, in 184 which the agent may walk around and ap185 proach the object to Push/Pull. Each 12– 186 cycle ‘petal’ represents a 12–step choreog187 raphy wherein the agent pushes and pulls 188 the object around in a 4–cycle in the grid189 world. In this example, the state complex is 190 the state graph, since there are no possible 191 commuting moves.

Figure 4: State complex (left) of a 3 _×_ 3 gridworld with one agent and one object (right). The darker vertex in the state complex represents the state shown in the gridworld state on the right. Edges in the state complex are coloured according to their generator – orange for Push/Pull and maroon for Move. Grey circles which group states where the ball is static have been added to illustrate the different scales of geometry.
- 192 The examples discussed thus far all have 193 planar state graphs. Planarity does not hold 194 in general – indeed, the _n_ –cube graph for

- 195 _n ≥_ 4 is non-planar, and a state graph can contain _n_ –cubes if the gridworld has _n_ agents and sufficient 196 space to move around. It is tempting to think that the state complex of a gridworld with more agents 197 should therefore look quite different to one with fewer agents. However, Figure 5 shows this may 198 not always be the case: there is a symmetry induced by swapping all `‘agent’` labels with `‘floor’` 199 labels.

199

Figure 5: State complex (centre) of a 3 _×_ 3 gridworld with three agents (left) and six agents (right). They share the same state complex due to the `‘agent’` _↔_ `‘floor’` label inversion symmetry.

## 200 **4 Dancing with myself**

201 The state complex of a gridworld with _n_ agents can be thought of as a discrete analogue of the 202 configuration space of _n_ points on the 2D–plane. However, there is a problem with this analogy: 203 there can be ‘holes’ created by 4–cycles in the state complex where a single agent walks in a small 204 square-shaped dance by itself, as shown in Figure 6.

205 The presence of these holes would suggest something meaningful about the underlying gridworld’s 206 intrinsic topology, e.g., something obstructing the agent’s movement at that location in the gridworld 207 that the agent must move around. In reality, the environment is essentially a (discretised) 2D–plane 208 with nothing blocking the agent from traversing those locations. Indeed, these ‘holes’ are uninteresting

5

Figure 6: State complex of a 2 _×_ 2 gridworld with one agent under the original definition of Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson [AG04, GP07] (left) and with our modification (right). The blue shading is a filled in square indicating a _dance_ .

209 topological quirks which arise due to the representation of the gridworld as a graph. We therefore 210 deviate from the original definition of state complexes by Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson [AG04, GP07] 211 and choose to fill in these ‘dance’ 4–cycles with squares.[1]

212 Formally, we define a **dance** _δ_ to comprise the following data:

213

- the support _SUP_ ( _δ_ ) given by a 2 _×_ 2 subgrid in the gridworld,
- 214 • four local states defined on _SUP_ ( _δ_ ), each consisting of exactly one agent label and three 215 floor labels, and
- 216 • four Move generators, each of which transitions between two of the four local states (as in 217 Figure 6).

218 We say that _δ_ is _admissible_ at a state _s_ if _s|SUP_ ( _δ_ ) agrees with one of the four local states of _δ_ . 219 Moreover, these four local states are precisely the states that can be reached when we apply some 220 combination of the four constituent Moves. We do not define the trace of a dance, however, we may 221 view the trace of each of the four constituent Moves as subgraphs of _SUP_ ( _δ_ ).

222 The notion of commutativity can be extended to incorporate dancing. Suppose that we have a set 223 _{ϕ_ 1 _, . . . , ϕl, δ_ 1 _, . . . , δm}_ of _l_ admissible generators and _m_ admissible dances at a state _s_ . We say 224 that this set _commutes_ if the supports of its elements are pairwise disjoint. When this holds, there 225 are 2 _[l]_[+2] _[m]_ possible states that can be obtained by applying some combination of the generators and 226 dances to _s_ : there are two choices of local state for each _ϕi_ , and four for each _δj_ . We capture this 227 extended notion of commutativity by attaching additional cubes to the state complex to form our 228 modified state complex.

229 **Definition 4.1** (Modified state complex) **.** The _modified state complex S[′]_ of a gridworld is the cube 230 complex obtained by filling in the state graph _S_[(1)] with higher dimensional cubes whenever there 231 is a set of commuting moves or dances. Specifically, whenever a set of 2 _[l]_[+2] _[m]_ states are related by 232 a commuting set of _l_ generators and _m_ dances, we add an _n_ –cube having the given set of states 233 as its vertices, where _n_ = _l_ + 2 _m_ . Each of the 2 _n_ faces of such an _n_ –cube is identified with an 234 ( _n −_ 1)–cube obtained by either disallowing a generator _ϕi_ and choosing one of its two local states, 235 or replacing a dance _δj_ with one of its four constituent Moves.

236 Our modification removes uninteresting topology. This can be observed by examining 4–cycles in _S[′]_ . 237 On the one hand, some 4–cycles are trivial (they can be ‘filled in’): _dancing-with-myself_ 4–cycles, 238 and _commuting moves_ (two agents moving back and forth) 4–cycles (which were trivial under the 239 original definition). These represent trivial movements of agents relative to one another. On the other 240 hand, there is a non-trivial 4–cycle in the state complex for two agents in a 2 _×_ 2 room, as can be seen 241 in the centre of Figure 3 (here, no dancing is possible so the modified state complex is the same as the 242 original). This 4–cycle represents the two agents moving half a ‘revolution’ relative to one another –
> 1Ghrist and Peterson themselves ask if there could be better ways to complete the state graph to a higherdimensional object with better properties (Question 6.4 in [GP07]).

6

- 243 indeed, performing this twice would give a full revolution. (There are three other non-trivial 4–cycles, 244 topologically equivalent to this central one, that also achieve the half-revolution.)

245 In a more topological sense[2] , by filling in such squares and higher dimensional cubes, our state 246 complexes capture the non-trivial, essential relative movements of the agents. This can be used 247 to study the braiding or mixing of agents, and also allows us to consider path-homotopic paths as 248 ‘essentially’ the same. One immediate difference this creates with the original state complexes is a 249 loss of symmetries like those shown in Figure 5, since there is no label inversion for a dance when 250 other agents are crowding the dance-floor.

## 251 **5 Gromov’s Link Condition**

252 The central geometric characteristic of Abrams, Ghrist, & Peterson’s state complexes is that they 253 are _non-positively curved_ (NPC). Indeed, this local geometric condition is conducive for developing 254 efficient algorithms for computing geodesics. However, with our modified state complexes, this NPC 255 geometry is no longer guaranteed – we test for this on a vertex-by-vertex basis using a classical 256 geometric result due to Gromov (see also Theorem 5.20 of [BH99] and [Sag14]).
257 **Theorem 5.1** (Gromov’s Link Condition [Gro87]) **.** _A finite-dimensional cube complex is NPC if and_ 258 _only if the link of every vertex is a flag simplicial complex._

259 We provide a brief mathematical back260 ground on cube complexes and the finer 261 details of Gromov’s Link Condition in Ap262 pendix A.1. For our current purposes, it is 263 sufficient to know that under the Abrams, 264 Ghrist & Peterson setup, if _v_ is a state in _S_ 265 then the vertices of its link _lk_ ( _v_ ) represent 266 the possible admissible generators at _v_ . 267 Since cubes in _S_ are associated with com268 muting sets of generators, each simplex in 269 _lk_ ( _v_ ) represents a set of commuting gener270 ators. Gromov’s Link Condition for _lk_ ( _v_ ) 271 can be reinterpreted as follows: whenever 272 a set of admissible generators is _pairwise_ 273 commutative, then it is _setwise_ commuta274 tive. Using this, it is straightforward for 275 Abrams, Ghrist & Peterson to verify that 276 this always holds for their state complexes 277 (see Theorem 4.4 of [GP07]).

278 For our modified states complexes, the sit279 uation is not as straightforward. The key 280 issue is that our cubes do not only arise 281 from commuting generators – we must 282 take dances into account. Indeed, when 283 attempting to prove that Gromov’s Link 284 Condition holds, we discovered some very 285 simple gridworlds where it actually fails; 286 see Figure 7 and Appendix A.4.

Figure 7: The two situations which lead to failure of Gromov’s Link Condition in multi-agent gridworlds. Maroon arrows indicate admissible moves and blue squares indicate admissible dances. Note that in the links (bottom row), the triangle is missing in the left example, while the (solid) tetrahedron is missing in the right (however, all 2D faces are present). This is due to the respective collections of moves and dances failing to commute – an agent interrupts the other’s dance (left) or two dances collide (right).

287 Failure of the Link Condition can indicate

288 available moves at some state that cannot be safely performed simultaneously and independently 289 without risking collisions between labels. Another interpretation of positive curvature in this context 290 is something akin to what real-time computer strategy games call ‘fog of war’ (distance-dependent 291 limiting of observations which extends from the player-controlled agents), and more specifically 292 the viewable distance from an agent’s line-of-sight. Such fog makes AI systems operating in such 293 environments particularly challenging, although remarkable success has been achieved in games like 294 StarCraft [VBC[+] 19].

294

> 2By considering the fundamental group.

7

295 Despite this apparent drawback, we nevertheless show that Figure 7 accounts for all the possible 296 failures of Gromov’s Link Condition in the setting of agent-only gridworlds[3] .
- 297 **Theorem 5.2** (Gromov’s Link Condition in the modified state complex) **.** _Let v be a vertex in the_ 298 _modified state complex S[′] of an agent-only gridworld. Then_
- 299 • _lk_ ( _v_ ) _satisfies Gromov’s Link Condition if and only if it has no empty_ 2 _–simplices nor_ 300 3 _–simplices_[4] _, and_
- 301 • _if lk_ ( _v_ ) _fails Gromov’s Link Condition then there exist a pair of agents whose positions_ 302 _differ by either a knight move or a_ 2 _–step bishop move (as in Figure 7)._

- 303 We provide a proof in Appendix A.2. Consequently, if the Link Condition fails at all, it must fail at 304 dimension 2 or 3. This can be interpreted as saying that we only need a bounded amount of foresight 305 to detect potential collisions: under fog-of-war, each agent needs a line-of-sight of only four moves.

- 306 Positive curvature could indicate collisions between any specified labels (e.g., objects), however, for 307 this interpretation to be valid we would need to carefully identify which other potential cycles in the 308 state complex ought to be filled in. Doing this in a ‘natural’ way is in itself a non-trivial task, and is 309 the subject of further investigation.

## 310

## **6 Experiments and applications**

311 Although our main contribution is theoretical, we conduct some small initial experiments to demon312 strate the type of information which can be captured in the geometry and topology (see Appendix 313 A.4). To run these experiments, we developed and used a custom Python-based tool (detailed in 314 Appendix A.3). Our focus on small rooms is largely expository, i.e., they are the simplest non-trivial 315 examples illustrating the key features we want to isolate, and naturally reoccur in all larger rooms. 316 Our intention is also to demonstrate a combinatorial explosion in the number of states. We don’t 317 recommend constructing the entire state complex in practical applications (indeed, to implement 318 addition of integers on a computer, it is infeasible and unnecessary to construct _all_ integers).

- 319 _Remark_ 6.1 _._ By a simple counting argument, one can deduce the total number of states in a gridworld. 320 For an agent-only gridworld with _n_ cells and _k_ agents, there is a total of � _nk_ � states. If there are _n_ 321 cells, _k_ agents, and _j_ objects, then there are � _nk_ �� _n−j k_ � states. Thus, even for a moderately sized 100
- 322 10 _×_ 10 room with 50 agents, there are � 50 � _≈_ 1 _._ 008 _×_ 10[29] vertices in the state complex.

- 323 By Theorem 5.2, checking if _lk_ ( _v_ ) satisfies Gromov’s Link Condition requires computing the link 324 only up to dimension 3 and then checking whether it is a flag complex; if not, we count the number 325 of empty simplices. Checking this for a given vertex in the state complex is not too computationally 326 demanding, however when a state complex has many vertices it becomes more difficult. In practical 327 applications, such as calculating collision-avoiding navigation routes, it is – again, by Theorem 328 5.2 – only necessary to construct a small local subcomplex. But perhaps even more importantly, to 329 detect potential collisions between agents, it is not even necessary to construct _lk_ ( _v_ ), since Theorem 330 5.2 provides a computational shortcut: just check for supports of knight or two-step bishop moves 331 between agents.

332 By using Gromov’s Link Condition, we can identify a precise measure of how far ahead agents ought 333 to look in order to safely proceed without fear of collisions. Appendix A.4 gives a summary analysis 334 of a 3 _×_ 3 room with varying numbers of agents. We noticed several symmetries. Commuting moves 335 and the number of states have a symmetry about 4 _._ 5 agents (due to the label-inversion symmetry as 336 previously illustrated in Figure 5). However, curiously, the number of dances has a symmetry about 337 3 _._ 5 agents. This difference leads to the asymmetrical distribution of positive curvature and failures 338 of Gromov’s Link Condition – which, while maximal for 3 agents as a proportion of total states, 339 exhibited the highest mean failure rate for 4 agents.

340 This shows that, heuristically, we expect most states to satisfy NPC (see Appendix A.4), and so 341 existing greedy algorithms [AOS12] for calculating geodesics will work well in most situations. 342 However, to implement an efficient, collision-free path-finding algorithm in our modified state 343 complexes, we need to add an additional check. Specifically, when we are near a potentially 344 dangerous state, we should implement a predefined ‘detour’ to avoid the collision, which can be done 345 on a local basis using the identified supports which lead to positive curvature (as in Figure 7).

## 346 **7 Conclusions and future directions**

347 This study presents novel applications of tools from geometric group theory and combinatorics to the 348 AI research community, opening new ways for recasting and analysing AI problems as geometric ones. 349 Using these tools, we show an example of how the intrinsic geometry of a task space serendipitously 350 embeds safety information and makes it possible to determine how far ahead in time an AI system 351 needs to observe to be guaranteed of avoiding dangerous actions.

352 Leike et al. [LMK[+] 17] show deep reinforcement learning agents cannot solve many AI safety prob353 lems specified on gridworlds, e.g., minimising unwanted side-effects or ensuring robustness to agent 354 self-modification. Having described the agent-only case in this study, there is now ripe opportunity to 355 account for positive curvature or other geometric features arising due to other labels or generators 356 (actions) present in specified AI safety problems, e.g., agents pushing/pulling objects, pressing 357 buttons, modifying their form or behaviour, rewards/punishments, opening/unlocking doors, etc.. By 358 considering _directed_ modified state complexes, irreversible actions can be captured by “invariant 359 subcomplexes” (i.e., you can’t escape from them), allowing geometric study of the tree/flowchart of 360 irreversible actions and related recurrence/transience. Braiding can be used to study route planning, 361 back-tracking, cooperation, assembly, and topological entropy in congestion [Ghr09]. Numerous 362 extensions are possible, allowing us to study and geometrically represent further problems with a 363 view to developing efficient, geometrically-inspired local algorithms without the need for training.

364 Do learning algorithms already implement such geometrically-inspired algorithms, the related ge365 ometry, or approximations thereof? To find out, we are investigating how modified state complexes 366 map to learned internal representations of neural networks trained to predict multi-agent gridworld 367 dynamics. This mapping connects the geometry and topology of a task space directly to optimisation 368 procedures and learning trajectories in latent representation spaces, highlighting unexpected topologi369 cal and geometric differences and opportunities for deeper insight and improvement of optimisation 370 procedures, in the spirit of [NZL20, ZZ22]. We can also compare biological optimisation processes 371 and internal representations of allocentric and egocentric navigation [Bur06, GHP[+] 22], and how this 372 interacts with the position of other agents [DJ18, SB20].

373 From a more mathematical perspective, state complexes of gridworlds give rise to an interesting class 374 of geometric spaces. It would be worthwhile to investigate their geometric and topological properties 375 to more deeply understand various aspects of multi-agent gridworlds. For example, for a gridworld 376 with _n_ agents in a sufficiently large room, we hypothesise that the modified state complex should be 377 a classifying space for the _n_ –strand braid group. This is clearly false when the room is packed full of 378 agents (in which case the state complex is a single point), so it may be fruitful to determine if there is 379 some ‘critical’ density at which a topological transition occurs.

380 Using the _failure_ of Gromov’s Link Condition in an essential way appears to be a relatively unexplored 381 approach. Indeed, much of the mathematical literature concerning cube complexes focusses on 382 showing that the Link Condition always holds. To our knowledge, the only other works which go 383 against this trend are [AG04], in which failure detects global disconnection of a metamorphic system, 384 and [BDT19], where failure detects non-trivial loops on topological surfaces. It would be interesting 385 to explore cube complexes arising in other settings where failure captures critical information.

386 A limitation of our work is that we have so far only explored very simple AI environments. Further 387 work is needed to expand the framework and results to more general, sophisticated, and real-world 388 environments. For this reason, although our work provides new geometric perspectives, data, 389 and potential algorithms for an important AI safety issue, we caution against hasty real-world 390 implementation of the main results. To avoid potential negative societal impacts, it would still be 391 important to perform rigorous checks and tests in application domains, since our results do not 392 directly extend to situations beyond which the stated assumptions hold.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents an intellectually ambitious and genuinely novel contribution: applying the framework of state complexes and Gromov's Link Condition from geometric group theory to multi-agent gridworlds, with the striking observation that failures of non-positive curvature coincide precisely with states admitting agent collisions. The conceptual bridge between combinatorial/geometric topology and AI safety is fresh, and the claimed characterization theorem (Theorem 5.2) — if fully substantiated — would be an elegant and potentially valuable result, offering a training-free, geometrically intrinsic approach to collision detection. The writing in the introduction is clear and the motivation is well-articulated, with appropriate positioning relative to prior work on reconfigurable systems, robotic motion planning, and geometric perspectives on deep learning. However, several significant concerns are already apparent. First, the practical significance of the contribution is underdeveloped: the paper frames this as a safety tool, yet gridworlds are highly idealized, and it is not clear how the geometric machinery scales or transfers to richer environments with continuous dynamics, stochasticity, or the richer gridworld elements (doors, keys, rewards) the authors themselves enumerate but do not address. Second, the "serendipitous" equivalence between Link Condition failure and collision potential, while appealing, risks being somewhat tautological — both conditions essentially encode that two agents' action supports overlap — and the paper will need to argue convincingly that the geometric reformulation yields genuinely new algorithmic or analytical leverage beyond what a direct combinatorial check provides. Third, the intended NeurIPS audience will likely find the mathematical prerequisites (cube complexes, NPC geometry, Gromov's condition) demanding, and the introduction would benefit from a concrete worked example and an explicit, early statement of the algorithmic or empirical payoff. Finally, the absence (in this excerpt) of experimental validation or comparison with existing multi-agent collision-detection baselines raises questions about whether the contribution will be evaluated as a theoretical proof-of-concept or as a practical method — a distinction the authors should sharpen.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for number of states with objects
comments.quote: If there are _n_ 321 cells, _k_ agents, and _j_ objects, then there are  _nk_  _n−j k_  states.
comments.explanation: I initially tried to parse this through the OCR. The intended formula for placing k agents and j objects on n distinguishable cells (with each cell holding at most one) should be C(n,k) * C(n-k, j) — first choose cells for agents, then cells for objects from the remaining n-k cells. The text appears to write C(n,k) * C(n-j, k), which swaps j and k in the second binomial and gives the wrong count. Even allowing for OCR mangling, the second binomial should involve (n-k) choose j (or equivalently (n-k) choose k if agents and objects counts were swapped, but here k is agents and j is objects). This is a real combinatorial error in the counting argument, though it's in a Remark and doesn't affect the main theorem.

comments.title: Hypothesis about classifying space for braid group lacks justification
comments.quote: for a gridworld with _n_ agents in a sufficiently large room, we hypothesise that the modified state complex should be a classifying space for the _n_ –strand braid group.
comments.explanation: This is presented as a future-directions hypothesis, which is appropriate framing. However, a careful reader might note that the modified state complex is a cube complex of configurations of n distinguishable-looking-but-interchangeable agents on a grid; the classifying space for the n-strand braid group is the unordered configuration space of n points in the plane (K(B_n,1)). Since agents in this paper carry identical 'agent' labels (the authors state 'We do not distinguish between multiple instances of the same label'), the states already correspond to unordered configurations, which is consistent with the braid group (rather than the pure braid group). This is a minor framing concern: the hypothesis is plausible but the paper does not discuss why dances (which fill in single-agent 4-cycles) plus commuting squares should yield asphericity or the correct fundamental group in the limit. Since this is explicitly flagged as a hypothesis for future work, it is acceptable, but the claim 'clearly false when the room is packed full of agents (in which case the state complex is a single point)' is slightly imprecise — a fully packed room has exactly one state only if every cell is occupied by an agent (with no floor); otherwise there may still be multiple states. Minor.

comments.title: Claim about 'most states' satisfying NPC is heuristic without support in shown passage
comments.quote: This shows that, heuristically, we expect most states to satisfy NPC (see Appendix A.4), and so existing greedy algorithms [AOS12] for calculating geodesics will work well in most situations.
comments.explanation: The passage begins 'This shows that...' but the referent of 'This' is in prior text not shown. The claim is hedged with 'heuristically' and points to Appendix A.4 for support, so the framing is defensible. A concern is that 'most states satisfy NPC' is a quantitative claim whose precise meaning (e.g., asymptotic density as grid size grows, fraction in a specific gridworld) is not specified here. Given it is explicitly heuristic and deferred to an appendix, this is a minor framing issue rather than a real error, but a reader could be misled into thinking a formal statement has been proved.

comments.title: Commutativity condition asymmetric in i, j
comments.quote: _commutes_ if _SUP_ ( _ϕi_ ) _∩ TR_ ( _ϕj_ ) = _∅_ for all _i ̸_ = _j_
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this is just the standard Abrams–Ghrist–Peterson definition. Earlier on line 114, the text says 'If the trace of an admissible generator ϕ1 is disjoint from the support of another admissible generator ϕ2, then ϕ2 remains admissible at ϕ1[s]' — this is the asymmetric, one-direction condition. The general definition then correctly quantifies over all i ≠ j, which makes the condition symmetric in practice (since swapping i and j gives SUP(ϕj) ∩ TR(ϕi) = ∅ as well). So the stated condition does require both SUP(ϕi) ∩ TR(ϕj) = ∅ AND SUP(ϕj) ∩ TR(ϕi) = ∅. This matches the AGP definition and is consistent; no issue, I was momentarily confused but resolved on reflection.

comments.title: Cube dimension count in Definition 4.1 conflicts with face identification
comments.quote: Each of the 2 _n_ faces of such an _n_ –cube is identified with an ( _n −_ 1)–cube obtained by either disallowing a generator _ϕi_ and choosing one of its two local states, or replacing a dance _δj_ with one of its four constituent Moves.
comments.explanation: At first I accepted this as a natural extension of the original state complex construction. On closer reading, there's an inconsistency in the face count. An n-cube has 2n codimension-1 faces. For an n-cube with n = l + 2m vertices formed by l generators and m dances: disallowing a generator gives 2 faces (one per local state), contributing 2l faces; replacing a dance with one of its four constituent Moves should give each dance contributing some number of faces. A dance's 4 local states arranged as a 4-cycle means that 'replacing a dance with one of its four constituent Moves' gives 4 choices per dance, contributing 4m faces. Total: 2l + 4m = 2(l+2m) = 2n. So the arithmetic works out. However, the description is ambiguous about what the resulting (n-1)-cube looks like — when you 'replace a dance δj with one of its four constituent Moves', the resulting face is an (n-1)-cube formed from (l+1) generators and (m-1) dances, which has l + 1 + 2(m-1) = l + 2m - 1 = n - 1 vertices-dimension, so this is consistent. On reflection the count is actually correct, though the prose is terse. Flagging as minor for the ambiguity: a reader might wonder whether 'choosing one of its two local states' counts as one face or two — the text could be read as giving 1 face per generator (total l + 4m ≠ 2n).

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially original bridge between state complexes from geometric group theory and multi-agent gridworld safety, and the abstract/introduction communicate an appealing high-level thesis: that failures of Gromov’s Link Condition in a modified state complex correspond to dangerous/collision-prone configurations. The writing is generally readable and motivating, especially for an AI audience unfamiliar with cube complexes, and the paper does a good job situating gridworlds as a simple domain where geometric structure may be useful. However, the central claims currently appear quite strong relative to the evidence visible in the opening: terms such as “dangerous,” “undesirable,” and “guaranteed safety limitations” need precise formal definitions, and the asserted exact correspondence between link-condition failures and collision states will need careful qualification, proof, and discussion of scope. The paper should also clarify why the proposed “braiding” modification is necessary, how it differs formally from the Abrams–Ghrist–Peterson construction, and whether the resulting geometric defects reveal genuinely new safety information beyond standard graph-based collision checking. For a NeurIPS-style audience, the exposition may be mathematically dense early on, and the transition from geometric topology to practical AI safety could be strengthened with concrete examples, algorithms, computational complexity, and empirical demonstrations. The most significant issue is likely not the mathematical framing itself, which is promising, but the need to substantiate its relevance: the authors should make explicit what problems their method solves better than existing planning/collision-detection methods, what assumptions are required, and how the theory scales beyond toy gridworlds.

comments.title: Safety and lookahead claims overstate the local collision-pattern result
comments.quote:
Serendipitously, we discover these failures occur exactly where undesirable or dangerous states appear in the gridworld.

Consequently, if the Link Condition fails at all, it must fail at dimension 2 or 3. This can be interpreted as saying that we only need a bounded amount of foresight to detect potential collisions: under fog-of-war, each agent needs a line-of-sight of only four moves.

Using these tools, we show an example of how the intrinsic geometry of a task space serendipitously embeds safety information and makes it possible to determine how far ahead in time an AI system needs to observe to be guaranteed of avoiding dangerous actions.
comments.explanation: The paper appears to prove a much more specific result: in the gridworld model studied, failures of the link condition correspond to particular local relative placements of agents that indicate potential collision risk. That supports a local geometric warning criterion. However, the wording sometimes broadens this into claims about detecting undesirable or dangerous states generally, line-of-sight under fog-of-war, or guaranteed avoidance of dangerous actions by an AI system. Those stronger interpretations would require additional formal assumptions about the meaning of danger, visibility and occlusion, action timing, control authority, adversarial behavior, and whether the observation radius is sufficient for the relevant policy problem. A safer formulation would restrict the claim to the specified class of potential collision configurations and to local radius checks in the formal gridworld model.

comments.title: Generality of the claimed action model is not justified by the definitions shown
comments.quote: However, our approach is purely geometric, requires no training, and can accommodate many conceivable types of actions and inter-actions, not just simple movements.
comments.explanation: The reconfigurable-systems framework is flexible, so the claim may be reasonable at the level of representability. But the paper’s formal setup specializes to a small set of labels and later to two concrete generator types, Move and Push/Pull. The distinctive dance-square modification is also closely tied to 2-by-2 single-agent move cycles. Thus the sentence could mislead readers into thinking that the collision/danger-detection theorem via Gromov’s Link Condition has been established for many action and interaction types. The paper should distinguish between actions the framework can encode in principle and the specific generators for which the present geometric characterization is proved.

comments.title: Construction procedure only specifies squares, not the full cubical structure
comments.quote:
From the state graph, we construct the state complex by first finding all 4–cycles in the state graph.

Then, by examining the states involved, we can determine whether a given 4–cycle bounds a square representing a pair of commuting moves.
comments.explanation: Definition 2.3 says that an n-cube should be added for any set of n commuting admissible generators, and later use of Gromov’s Link Condition depends on the full cube structure, not just the graph and valid squares. The construction passage describes how to detect 4-cycles that bound squares, but it does not state the subsequent procedure for attaching higher-dimensional cubes. Attaching all valid squares gives the 2-skeleton, but it does not determine which collections of pairwise commuting moves form 3-cubes and higher cubes unless an explicit completion rule or enumeration of commuting generator sets is supplied. Because links, flagness, and NPC checks depend on higher-dimensional cells, the computational method is under-specified.

comments.title: State-graph variable G reuses notation already assigned to the gridworld graph
comments.quote: To compute the state complex of a (finite) gridworld, we first initialise an empty graph G and an empty ‘to-do’ list L. As input, we take a chosen state of the gridworld to form the first vertex of G
comments.explanation: Earlier, G denotes the graph whose vertices are gridworld cells. Here G is reintroduced as the graph being built by breadth-first search, namely the state graph or a connected component of it. Since the algorithm involves both the underlying cell graph and the state graph, using the same symbol for both can obscure whether vertices are cells or states and where generators are being applied. Using the already introduced notation S^{(1)} for the state graph would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: Dance-square terminology blurs sequences, compatibility, and simultaneous independent moves
comments.quote:
Moreover, these four local states are precisely the states that can be reached when we apply some combination of the four constituent Moves.

Failure of the Link Condition can indicate available moves at some state that cannot be safely performed simultaneously and independently without risking collisions between labels.
comments.explanation: The intended construction is understandable: a dance fills the 4-cycle obtained by moving one agent around the four cells of a 2-by-2 subgrid. But the phrase “some combination of the four constituent Moves” is ambiguous in the surrounding reconfigurable-system context, where combinations usually refer to mutually commuting admissible generators. The four moves around a 2-by-2 square are not all simultaneously admissible or mutually commuting; reaching the opposite corner requires a sequence. This also affects the later operational interpretation of link failures. Once dance squares are added, not every edge in a link represents simultaneous independent primitive moves by distinct agents; some represent compatibility introduced by dance fillings between alternative moves of the same agent. The paper should distinguish ordinary commuting-generator cubes from dance squares and avoid interpreting all resulting link simplices as simultaneous independent actions.

comments.title: Sparse local failures do not by themselves justify CAT(0)/NPC geodesic algorithms
comments.quote: This shows that, heuristically, we expect most states to satisfy NPC (see Appendix A.4), and so existing greedy algorithms [AOS12] for calculating geodesics will work well in most situations.
comments.explanation: The intended heuristic is plausible: if non-flag links are rare, NPC-based algorithms may still be useful in practice. However, CAT(0)/NPC geodesic algorithms rely on global non-positive curvature properties, not merely on most vertex links being flag. Even a small number of non-flag links can destroy the uniqueness and convexity properties used by such algorithms, and it is not shown that paths avoiding bad vertices inherit the required structure. The phrasing “most states satisfy NPC” also compresses a global complex property into a local vertex-link condition. A more precise statement would say that most vertex links appear to satisfy the flag condition and that CAT(0)-based methods are being proposed as a heuristic rather than justified by the theorem.

comments.title: Local positive-curvature supports do not automatically provide a collision-free detour
comments.quote: Specifically, when we are near a potentially dangerous state, we should implement a predefined ‘detour’ to avoid the collision, which can be done on a local basis using the identified supports which lead to positive curvature (as in Figure 7).
comments.explanation: Theorem 5.2 identifies small local configurations that cause link-condition failure, so these supports are natural warning signs. But detecting a local warning pattern does not by itself prove that a predefined local detour exists or can always be implemented safely. Detour existence may depend on walls, boundary cells, objects, other agents, congestion, and the global connectivity of the safe state space. The paper should either present an explicit detour policy with assumptions under which it is valid, or weaken the claim to say that these supports can inform local avoidance heuristics.

comments.title: Directed irreversible dynamics and “invariant subcomplexes” are underdefined
comments.quote: By considering directed modified state complexes, irreversible actions can be captured by “invariant subcomplexes” (i.e., you can’t escape from them), allowing geometric study of the tree/flowchart of irreversible actions and related recurrence/transience.
comments.explanation: As a future direction, this is a reasonable idea, but the mathematical object is not specified. In an undirected cube complex, a subcomplex is a cubical/topological object; in a directed transition system, “you can’t escape” usually means a forward-invariant or absorbing set of states. It is not automatic that an absorbing set induced by irreversible actions is a subcomplex of the underlying cubical complex, and it is unclear what directed cubes, faces, and higher-dimensional admissible cells would be. Recurrence and transience also normally require a probabilistic or dynamical rule, not just a directed complex. The proposal would benefit from definitions before making these claims.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially original bridge between state complexes from geometric group theory and multi-agent gridworld safety, and the abstract/introduction communicate an appealing high-level thesis: that failures of Gromov’s Link Condition in a modified state complex correspond to dangerous/collision-prone configurations. The writing is generally readable and motivating, especially for an AI audience unfamiliar with cube complexes, and the paper does a good job situating gridworlds as a simple domain where geometric structure may be useful. However, the central claims currently appear quite strong relative to the evidence visible in the opening: terms such as “dangerous,” “undesirable,” and “guaranteed safety limitations” need precise formal definitions, and the asserted exact correspondence between link-condition failures and collision states will need careful qualification, proof, and discussion of scope. The paper should also clarify why the proposed “braiding” modification is necessary, how it differs formally from the Abrams–Ghrist–Peterson construction, and whether the resulting geometric defects reveal genuinely new safety information beyond standard graph-based collision checking. For a NeurIPS-style audience, the exposition may be mathematically dense early on, and the transition from geometric topology to practical AI safety could be strengthened with concrete examples, algorithms, computational complexity, and empirical demonstrations. The most significant issue is likely not the mathematical framing itself, which is promising, but the need to substantiate its relevance: the authors should make explicit what problems their method solves better than existing planning/collision-detection methods, what assumptions are required, and how the theory scales beyond toy gridworlds.

comments.title: Abstract overstates the scope of what Gromov-link failures identify
comments.quote: With this modification, 11 the state complexes may exhibit geometric defects (failure of _Gromov’s Link Condi-_ 12 _tion_ ). Serendipitously, we discover these failures occur exactly where undesirable 13 or dangerous states appear in the gridworld.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended headline: later in the introduction the authors clarify that the relevant failures correspond to states in which agents can potentially collide, and they point forward to Theorem 5.2. That makes the claim plausible if “dangerous” is being used as shorthand for a particular collision-risk condition in their formal model. What remains problematic is the broader wording here: “undesirable or dangerous states” sounds like an arbitrary semantic class of bad states in gridworlds, including rewards, punishments, locks, hazards, objects in bad positions, etc., many of which are mentioned nearby as possible gridworld features but are not yet part of the formal labels/actions. Gromov’s Link Condition is a local cubical-complex condition; without a formal definition of “dangerous” restricted to potential collision configurations, the word “exactly” overclaims the result. A safer formulation would say that, in the gridworlds studied here, these failures exactly detect a specified class of potential collision states.

comments.title: Generality of the claimed action model is not justified by the definitions shown
comments.quote: However, 49 our approach is purely geometric, requires no training, and can accommodate many conceivable types 50 of actions and inter-actions, not just simple movements.
comments.explanation: The underlying reconfigurable-systems framework is indeed quite flexible, so I see why the authors make this point. The formal setup immediately following, however, specializes the gridworld to a small set of labels and later to two concrete generator types, Move and Push/Pull. The paper’s distinctive modification—the dance square filling used to capture braiding—is also closely tied to 2-by-2 single-agent move cycles. Thus the sentence is potentially misleading if read as saying that the collision/danger-detection result via Gromov’s Link Condition has already been established for many kinds of actions or interactions. It may be true at the level of representability in a reconfigurable system, but the geometric characterization of danger appears to require additional action-specific analysis. The claim would be clearer if it distinguished “the framework can encode” many actions from “the present theorem applies to” the particular generators studied.

comments.title: Construction procedure only specifies squares, not the full cubical structure
comments.quote:
From the state graph, we construct the state complex by first finding all 4–cycles in the state graph.

- 166 Then, by examining the states involved, we can determine whether a given 4–cycle bounds a square 167 representing a pair of commuting moves.
comments.explanation: The preceding Definition 2.3 correctly says that an \(n\)-cube should be added for any set of \(n\) commuting admissible generators, and the later discussion of Gromov’s Link Condition will depend on knowing the full cube structure, not just the state graph. I initially read “by first finding all 4–cycles” as perhaps only the first stage of a fuller construction, but the passage never states the subsequent step for detecting and attaching higher-dimensional cubes. Attaching all valid squares gives the 2-skeleton, but it does not by itself specify which collections of pairwise commuting moves form 3-cubes and higher cubes, unless an additional flag/completion rule or explicit enumeration of commuting generator sets is imposed. This matters because links, flagness, and NPC checks require knowing higher-dimensional simplices corresponding to higher-dimensional cubes. The later sentence that higher-dimensional cubes are “easily computed and visualised if desired” helps for visualization, but it does not make the computational method reproducible.

comments.title: State-graph variable \(G\) reuses notation already assigned to the gridworld graph
comments.quote: - 151 To compute the state complex of a (finite) gridworld, we first initialise an empty graph _G_ and an 152 empty ‘to-do’ list _L_ . As input, we take a chosen state of the gridworld to form the first vertex of _G_
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same passage, \(G\) is the graph whose vertices are gridworld cells. Here \(G\) is reintroduced as the graph being built by breadth-first search, i.e. the state graph or a connected component of it. I checked whether the authors consistently switch to this new meaning locally; they do, since the output graph is later called a component of the true state graph. Still, this is a genuine notation collision: the algorithm involves both the underlying grid graph and the state graph, and using \(G\) for both can mislead a reader about whether admissible generators are being applied on the cell graph or whether vertices are states. Since Definition 2.2 already denotes the state graph by \(S^{(1)}\), using that notation here would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: “Two generators” appears to mean two generator schemas, not two actual generators
comments.quote:
- 103 We do not distinguish between multiple instances of the same label. We consider two generators:

- 104 • **Push/Pull.** An agent adjacent to an object is allowed to push/pull the object if there is an 105 unoccupied floor cell straight in front of the object/straight behind the agent; and

- 106 • **Move.** An agent is allowed to move to a neighbouring unoccupied floor cell.
comments.explanation: Under the formal definition just given, a generator includes a specific support subgraph and local states. In a gridworld with many adjacent cell pairs and many length-three rows/columns, there are therefore many individual Move and Push/Pull generators, one for each relevant support and orientation/local pattern. The later sentence referring to “the two generator types” suggests the intended meaning is clear: Move and Push/Pull are schemas or families of generators. However, the formal reconfigurable-system data require the actual set of generators, and the passage does not quite spell out the instantiation over all embedded adjacent pairs and length-three paths. This is mostly a terminology/precision issue, but it could matter for reproducing the state graph construction.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of “combination” for non-commuting dance moves
comments.quote: Moreover, these four local states are precisely the states that can be reached when we apply some combination of the four constituent Moves.
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear: a dance is meant to fill the 4-cycle obtained by moving a single agent around the four cells of a 2 × 2 subgrid, so the four local states should be the four possible positions of that agent in the square. What gave me pause is the phrase “apply some combination of the four constituent Moves.” In the Abrams–Ghrist–Peterson framework used earlier, “combination” naturally suggests applying a subset of mutually commuting admissible generators, but the four Move generators around a 2 × 2 square are not mutually admissible at once and do not commute. From any one local state, only two of the four moves are initially admissible, and reaching the opposite corner requires a sequence, not a subset/combination in the earlier sense. The statement is salvageable if “combination” means “some finite sequence of constituent Moves within the 2 × 2 support,” but as written it blurs an important distinction precisely at the point where dances are being introduced as new 2-dimensional cells rather than ordinary commuting-generator cubes.

comments.title: State graph is said to contain higher-dimensional cubes
comments.quote:
Planarity does not hold in general – indeed, the _n_ –cube graph for

- 195 _n ≥_ 4 is non-planar, and a state graph can contain _n_ –cubes if the gridworld has _n_ agents and sufficient 196 space to move around.
comments.explanation: I think the authors’ intended point is correct: with n sufficiently separated agents, the state graph can contain the 1-skeleton of an n-cube, namely the graph Q_n arising from n independent binary moves; since Q_n is non-planar for n ≥ 4, state graphs need not be planar. However, the sentence switches from the precise term “n-cube graph” to saying that the state graph can contain “n-cubes.” A graph cannot literally contain higher-dimensional cubes as cells; those belong to the state complex, while the state graph contains only their 1-skeleta. This is likely shorthand, but because the paragraph is specifically distinguishing state graphs, state complexes, and planarity, the wording could mislead a careful reader. Replacing “n-cubes” with “n-cube graphs” or “1-skeleta of n-cubes” would make the argument precise.

comments.title: Simultaneous-move interpretation is imprecise once dance squares are added
comments.quote:
Failure of the Link Condition can indicate

288 available moves at some state that cannot be safely performed simultaneously and independently 289 without risking collisions between labels.
comments.explanation: I see the intended intuition: an empty simplex in the link means that several local directions are pairwise compatible but do not extend to one higher-dimensional cube, and Figure 7 usefully ties this to collision-like interference between agents. However, in the modified complex not every edge in the link has the same operational meaning as in the Abrams–Ghrist–Peterson complex. In particular, a dance square joins the two outgoing Move directions of a single agent in a 2×2 block; those two moves are alternative first moves by the same agent, not moves that can literally be performed simultaneously and independently. Thus an empty triangle involving a dance edge is not simply a set of available primitive moves that all agents might execute at once. The statement is softened by “can indicate,” but a careful reader could still infer a stronger operational interpretation than the modified cube construction supports. A more precise phrasing would distinguish simultaneous independent generators from compatibility of cube directions introduced by dance fillings.

comments.title: Theorem phrases Gromov’s Link Condition as a property of a link
comments.quote: lk ( v ) satisfies Gromov’s Link Condition if and only if it has no empty 2 –simplices nor 300 3 –simplices[4] , and
comments.explanation: The intended meaning is clear from Theorem 5.1 and the following words: the link should be flag, and in this special class of modified gridworld links it suffices to rule out empty 2- and 3-simplices. Still, in standard cube-complex terminology Gromov’s Link Condition is a condition on the cube complex, namely that every vertex link is flag; the link itself does not usually “satisfy Gromov’s Link Condition,” it is flag or not flag. This is mostly terminological, and the equivalence with absence of empty simplices resolves the mathematical content, but the wording could be tightened to avoid conflating the condition on the complex with the flagness property of an individual link.

comments.title: “Line-of-sight of only four moves” is an underdefined consequence of the theorem
comments.quote: Consequently, if the Link Condition fails at all, it must fail at 304 dimension 2 or 3. This can be interpreted as saying that we only need a bounded amount of foresight 305 to detect potential collisions: under fog-of-war, each agent needs a line-of-sight of only four moves.
comments.explanation: The connection to Theorem 5.2 is plausible: the listed dangerous relative positions are a knight move or a two-step bishop move apart, whose grid/Manhattan separations are at most four. So the authors are not pulling the number out of nowhere. What remains somewhat underjustified is the translation from this local combinatorial statement to “line-of-sight” under fog-of-war. Line-of-sight usually suggests visibility constraints, possible occlusion, and a sensing model, none of which has been formalised here; Theorem 5.2 only says that any link-condition failure contains a pair of agents in one of two relative configurations. It does not by itself specify what an agent observes, whether obstacles/walls block observation, or whether seeing within graph distance four is both necessary and sufficient to classify the local link. This is acceptable as an informal interpretation, but it would be safer to phrase it as a radius-four local check in the agent-only gridworld model rather than as a line-of-sight guarantee.

comments.title: “States satisfy NPC” conflates a global complex property with a local link condition
comments.quote: This shows that, heuristically, we expect most states to satisfy NPC (see Appendix A.4)
comments.explanation: I understand the intended shorthand: by the preceding use of Gromov’s Link Condition, the authors mean that at most vertices the link is flag, so the modified complex is locally non-positively curved at those vertices. That context mostly resolves the concern. Still, in standard cube-complex terminology NPC is a property of the whole finite-dimensional cube complex, equivalent to every vertex link being flag, not a property that individual states/vertices themselves satisfy. A careful reader could read this as saying that a complex with a few bad vertices is somehow ‘mostly NPC’ in a way that preserves NPC conclusions. A more precise phrasing would be that “most vertex links satisfy the flag condition” or “most states are locally NPC.”

comments.title: Sparse local failures do not by themselves justify CAT(0)/NPC geodesic algorithms
comments.quote: This shows that, heuristically, we expect most states to satisfy NPC (see Appendix A.4), and so existing greedy algorithms [AOS12] for calculating geodesics will work well in most situations.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that if positive-curvature/link-condition failures are rare, one might hope NPC-based methods remain useful heuristically. However, the cited geodesic algorithms for CAT(0)/NPC cube complexes rely on global non-positive curvature properties, not merely on most vertex links being flag. Once the modified state complex has even a small number of non-flag links, uniqueness and convexity properties used by such algorithms may fail, and it is not immediate that paths avoiding the bad vertices inherit the needed structure. The statement is qualified with “heuristically” and “work well,” so it is not a formal theorem, but the implication from rare local failures to expected algorithmic performance is not justified in the passage.

comments.title: Local positive-curvature supports do not automatically provide a collision-free detour
comments.quote: Specifically, when we are near a potentially dangerous state, we should implement a predefined ‘detour’ to avoid the collision, which can be done on a local basis using the identified supports which lead to positive curvature (as in Figure 7).
comments.explanation: I see the intended connection: Theorem 5.2 identifies small local configurations, such as knight-move and two-step-bishop relative positions, that cause link-condition failure, so these supports are natural local warning signs. What remains unclear is the stronger algorithmic claim that a predefined local detour can be implemented to avoid collision. The existence of a detour can depend on boundary cells, walls/objects, other agents, congestion, and the global connectivity of the safe region. The identified support detects a potential obstruction, but it does not by itself specify a reproducible detour policy or prove that such a detour exists from every nearby state. This is especially relevant because the sentence is framed as an implementation step for an efficient collision-free path-finding algorithm.

comments.title: Guarantee about lookahead for avoiding dangerous actions is stronger than the shown results appear to support
comments.quote: Using these tools, we show an example of how the intrinsic geometry of a task space serendipitously embeds safety information and makes it possible to determine how far ahead in time an AI system needs to observe to be guaranteed of avoiding dangerous actions.
comments.explanation: The paper’s result, as summarized, does give a compelling local geometric characterization of certain potentially dangerous agent-only configurations: link-condition failures correspond to specific relative placements of pairs of agents. That supports a statement about local warning patterns or required local observation radius under the paper’s assumptions. The quoted claim is broader: it speaks of an AI system being guaranteed to avoid dangerous actions. Such a guarantee would require additional assumptions about action timing, control authority over other agents, policies/adversarial behavior, boundary conditions, and what counts as a dangerous action. The later limitation paragraph partially reins this in, but the conclusion here still overstates what follows from identifying positive-curvature supports in the modified state complex.

comments.title: Directed irreversible dynamics and “invariant subcomplexes” are underdefined
comments.quote: By considering _directed_ modified state complexes, irreversible actions can be captured by “invariant subcomplexes” (i.e., you can’t escape from them), allowing geometric study of the tree/flowchart of irreversible actions and related recurrence/transience.
comments.explanation: As a future direction, this is a reasonable and interesting idea: irreversible actions naturally suggest directed transition structures or absorbing regions. The concern is that the mathematical object is not specified enough for the statement as written. In an undirected cube complex, a subcomplex is a cubical/topological object; in a directed transition system, “you can’t escape” usually means a forward-invariant or absorbing set of states. It is not automatic that the absorbing state set induced by an irreversible action is a subcomplex of the underlying cubical complex, nor is it clear what directed cubes, faces, and admissible higher-dimensional cells are intended to be. Also, recurrence/transience normally requires a probabilistic or dynamical rule, not just a directed complex. Since this is explicitly framed as a future extension, the issue is minor, but a careful reader would need definitions before the proposed analysis is mathematically well specified.
